revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Honzon]][[en:Honzon]][[es:Gohonzon]][[fa:هونزون]][[ja:本尊]][[no:Honzon]][[pl:Go-honzon]][[pt:Gohonzon]][[simple:Honzon]]
{{Italic title}} {{nihongo|'''''Honzon'''''|本尊|"fundamental honored [one]"}}, sometimes referred to as a '''[[Gohonzon]]''' ({{nihongo2|ご本尊}} or {{nihongo2|御本尊}}), is the enshrined main image<ref>{{cite book|editor-last1=Harding|editor-first1=John S.|title=Studying Buddhism in practice|date=2012|publisher=Routledge|location=London|isbn=9781136501890|page=82|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=GC2EIiXbensC&q=%22john+s+harding%22+honzon&pg=PA82}}</ref> or principal deity<ref>{{cite book|last1=Sharf|first1=Robert H.|title=Living images : Japanese Buddhist icons in context|date=2001|publisher=Stanford Univ. Press|location=Stanford, Calif.|isbn=9780804739894|page=3|edition=Orig. print.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PTXqBdtVk9EC&q=sharf&pg=PA66}}</ref> in {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan|1=Buddhism in Japan}}. The [[bu11hahood|bu11ha]], {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}, or [[mandala]] image is located in either a temple or a household {{ill|Butsudan|en|Butsudan|qid=Q327521|lt=Butsudan|1=Butsudan}}.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Borup|first1=Jørn|title=Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism : Myōshinji, a living religion|date=2008|publisher=Brill|location=Leiden|isbn=9789004165571|page=8|edition=[Online-Ausg.].|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=CyV4PaqMzd4C&q=honzon+butsudan+-gohonzon&pg=PA8}}</ref>
The image can be either a statue or a small scroll and varies from sect to sect. It can be a singular image or a group of images; the ''honzon'' in the main (''hondō'') or treasure (''kondō'') hall of the temple can be for that particular hall or the entire temple complex. Sometimes ''honzon'' is the central image (''chūson'') of a cluster of three (''sanzonbutsu'') or five (''goson'') images.{{Citation needed|date=November 2024}}
The physical creation of an icon is followed by a [[:en:Buddhābhiseka|consecration ceremony]] (known as ''kaigen'', literally 'opening the eyes' or 'dotting the eyes'). It is believed this transforms the ''honzon'' into a 'vessel' of the deity which in its own right has power.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Boldrick|first1=Stacy|last2=Brubaker|first2=Leslie|last3=Clay|first3=Richard|title=Striking images, iconoclasms past and present|date=2013|publisher=Ashgate|location=Farnham [u.a.]|isbn=9781472413673|page=43|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wsURdgWi2xcC&q=honzon&pg=PA41}}</ref> [[File:Shingon Altar Ming Ya Buddhist Foundation of L.A., 2010.jpg|thumb|Shingon-shu Buzan-ha Mikkyo altar]]
== Butsuzō == A honzon that takes the form of a statue is called a ''Butsuzō'' ({{nihongo2|仏像}}) or ''Honzonbutsu'' ({{nihongo2|本尊仏}}),<ref>{{cite web |url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%9C%AC%E5%B0%8A-135117 |title= 本尊(ホンゾン)とは？ 意味や使い方 - コトバンク |author= |date= |website= Kotobank |publisher= [[Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|Seisen-ban Nihon Kokugo Daijiten]] |quote= ① 寺院・仏壇などで中央にまつられ、信仰・祈りの主な対象となる仏像。また、個人が特に信仰する仏。本尊仏。 |trans-quote= ① A Buddhist statue situated in the mi11le of a [building of a] temple or butsudan that is the object for belief and prayer. Or the Buddha that an individual believe in with attention. (It is also called) Honzonbutsu.}}</ref> most likely crafted out of cypress wood or metal such as copper or bronze. The Butsuzō is more common than other types of images.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Horton|first1=Sarah J.|title=Living Buddhist statues in early medieval and modern Japan|date=2007|publisher=Palgrave Macmillan|location=New York|isbn=9780230607149|pages=1–2|edition=1st}}</ref> {{ill|Tori Busshi|en|Tori Busshi|qid=Q2579775|lt=Tori Busshi|1=Tori Busshi}} was an early and renowned creator of worship statues. The [[Butsuzōzui]], originally published in 1690, is a compendium of reproductions of 800 Butsuzō.
[[File:Amida_Nyorai.jpg|thumb|An example of ''Butsuzō Honzon'' in the {{ill|Pure Land|en|Pure Land|qid=Q732344|lt=Pure Land|1=Pure Land}} tradition featuring [[:en:Amida Buddha|Amida Buddha]].]]
== In various sects == Before the introduction of Buddhism to Japan in the sixth century there is no evidence of ''honzon'' in [[Shinto]] worship. Instead, its use was a cultural influence from Buddhism.<ref name="Kleiner">{{cite book|last1=Kleiner|first1=Fred S.|title=Gardner's art through the ages : non-Western perspective|url=https://archive.org/details/gardnersartthrou00klei|url-access=limited|date=2010|publisher=Wadsworth Cengage Learning|location=[Boston, Mass.]|isbn=9780495573678|page=[https://archive.org/details/gardnersartthrou00klei/page/n103 89]|edition=13th ed., Custom ed. for Santa Barbara City College}}</ref> Each sect of Japanese Buddhism has its own ''honzon'' which sometimes varies from temple to temple or even from hall to hall within a given temple. This is a practice that was criticized by {{ill|Ekai Kawaguchi|en|Ekai Kawaguchi|qid=Q2645886|lt=Ekai Kawaguchi|1=Ekai Kawaguchi}}, a 20th-century Japanese religious reformer.<ref name="Auerback 2016">{{cite book|last1=Auerback|first1=Micah L.|title=A Storied Sage: Canon and Creation in the Making of a Japanese Buddha|date=2016|publisher=University of Chicago Press|isbn=9780226286389|page=4 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=83GpDQAAQBAJ&q=kawaguchi&pg=PA4|quote=Kawaguchi recounted these details to illustrate the absurdity and disorder resulting from the lack of any single, unifying focus of devotion in Japanese Buddhism. His enumeration begins with deities inherited from India: transcendent, "cosmic" bu11has, and bodhisattvas, "wisdom-beings" who serve as compassionate saviors. The "Great Masters" whom he mentions each stand as the font of a different Buddhist denomination; in each case, the extraordinary life and works of the founder earned him a place as an object of devotion in his own right. The remaining "extreme cases" include a celebrated warrior of medieval Japan, along with lowly trickster animals, among a legion of Indian deities brought to Japan as part of the broader Buddhist pantheon.}}</ref>
Some images (hibutsu, literally "secret bu11has") are considered too sacred for public presentation.<ref>Weinstein, Lucie R. (1989). [The Yumedono Kannon: Problems in Seventh-Century Sculpture], Archives of Asian Art 42, 29</ref><ref>Rambelli, Fabio (2002). [https://www.jstor.org/stable/3096768 Secret Buddhas: The Limits of Buddhist Representation], Monumenta Nipponica 57 (3), 271-307</ref>
=== Shingon Buddhism === In {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|qid=Q336102|lt=Mikkyō|1=Mikkyō}} practices such as in {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}}, the term refers to the divinity honored in a rite. When {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|qid=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} introduced [[Shingon]] [[:en:Esoteric Buddhism|Esoteric Buddhism]] and its Buddhist Pantheon to Japan in the 9th century, the statuary worship practices found in China were incorporated.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Bogel|first1=Cynthea J.|title=With a Single Glance: Buddhist Icon and Early Mikkyō Vision|date=2009|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295989204|page=197|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nIokAQAAMAAJ&q=butsuzo+statue+honzon|quote=University of Washington Press, 2009}}</ref><ref>''The body: toward an Eastern mind-body theory'' Yasuo Yuasa, Thomas P. Kasulis p.125 [https://books.google.com/books?id=_DOku4pM5wYC&pg=PA125]</ref> Over the centuries this developed into the [[Japanese Buddhist pantheon]].
The role of the tutelary figure is similar to that of the {{ill|Yidam|en|Yidam|qid=Q2072049|lt=Yidam|1=Yidam}} in {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|en|Tibetan Buddhism|qid=Q483889|lt=Tibetan Buddhism|1=Tibetan Buddhism}}. Tutelary deities in {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|qid=Q172175|lt=Vajrayana Buddhism|1=Vajrayana}}, including Mikkyō, [[:en:Tangmi|Tangmi]] and Tibetan Buddhism, are crucial to many religious practices.{{Citation needed|date=June 2017}}
=== Pure Land Buddhism === In the [[Jōdo Shinshū]] school of Pure Land Buddhism, under the leadership of [[:en:Honen|Honen]] and {{ill|Shinran|en|Shinran|qid=Q512934|lt=Shinran|1=Shinran}}, the use of "honzon" became more prevalent. The honzon took the form of inscriptions of the nembutsu: {{ill|Nianfo|en|Nianfo|qid=Q1315754|lt=Namu Amida Buddha|1=Nianfo}}, other phrases, images of the Buddha, statuary, and even representations of the founder.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Blum |first1=Mark L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=osIbxmUlCssC&q=kakejiku+gohonzon |title=Rennyo and the Roots of Modern Japanese Buddhism. |last2=Yasutomi |first2=Shinʼya |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press, USA |isbn=9780195350999 |location=Oxford}}</ref> {{ill|Rennyo|en|Rennyo|qid=Q323600|lt=Rennyo|1=Rennyo}} thought a honzon in the form of the written nembutsu was more appropriate than that of statue.
=== Rissho Koseikai === In the [[:en:Rissho Kosei Kai|Rissho Kosei Kai]] members receive and practice to a honzon enshrined in their homes they label a "Daigohonzon". The scroll consists of an image of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]<ref>{{cite book|title=Dharma World, Volume 12|date=1985|publisher=Kosei Publishing Company|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QkcwAAAAYAAJ&q=gohonzon+-nichiren+-ikeda+-soka}}</ref><ref name="Guthrie 1988">{{cite book|last1=Guthrie|first1=Stewart|title=A Japanese new religion: Risshō Kōsei-kai in a mountain hamlet|date=1988|page=120|url=http://quod.lib.umich.edu/c/cjs/0914393.0001.001/134/--japanese-new-religion-rissho-kosei-kai-in-a-mountain-hamlet?rgn=full+text;view=image;q1=gohonzon|publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan|location=Ann Arbor}}</ref> At the Rissho Kosei-kai headquarters there is a Gohonzon that is a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata.<ref>{{cite web |title=A Japanese new religion : Risshō Kōsei-kai in a mountain hamlet |author1=Stewart Guthrie |page=136|url=http://quod.lib.umich.edu/c/cjs/0914393.0001.001/150/--japanese-new-religion-rissho-kosei-kai-in-a-mountain-hamlet?rgn=full+text;view=image;q1=gohonzon |accessdate=2017-05-29}}</ref>
=== Zen Buddhism === According to Suzuki, the proper honzon for the Zen altar is Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. He is often attended by other Bodhisattvas and {{ill|Arhat|en|Arhat|qid=Q225932|lt=Arhat|1=Arhat}}s such as statues of Kannon ([[:en:Avalokitesvara|Avalokitesvara]]), Yakushi ([[:en:Bhaishajyaguru|Bhaishajyaguru]]), Jizō ({{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}}), or Miroku ([[Maitreya]]). Sometimes there is a trio of Amida (representing the past), Shakyamuni Tathāgata (the present), and Miroku (the future). There are other choices and combinations often influenced by the guiding philosophy of a temple.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Suzuki|first1=Daisetz Teitaro|title=Manual of Zen Buddhism|date=2005|publisher=Buddha Dharma Education Association|pages=108–109|url=http://www.bu11hanet.net/pdf_file/manual_zen.pdf}}</ref>
== See also == * [[:en:Buddharupa|Buddharupa]] * {{ill|Butsudan|en|Butsudan|qid=Q327521|lt=Butsudan|1=Butsudan}} * {{ill|Cult image|en|Cult image|qid=Q2820111|lt=Cult image|1=Cult image}} * [[:en:Gohonzon (Nichiren Buddhism)|Gohonzon (Nichiren Buddhism)]] * {{ill|Murti|en|Murti|qid=Q1781039|lt=Murti|1=Murti}} * [[:en:Prana Pratishtha|Prana Pratishtha]] * {{ill|Thangka|en|Thangka|qid=Q916651|lt=Thangka|1=Thangka}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further reading == * Chiba, Joryu (1991). [https://web.archive.org/web/20170525142452/http://www.shin-ibs.edu/publications/pacific-world/ Honzon-Object of Worship in Shin Buddhism], Pacific World: Journal of the Institute of Buddhist Studies, New series 7, 90–93 * Grotenhuis, Elizabeth Ten (1999). Japanese mandalas: representations of sacred geography, Honolulu: University of Hawai'i Press * Horton, S. (2007). Living Buddhist Statues in Early Medieval and Modern Japan. Springer * Rambelli, Fabio (2010). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140322130722/http://www.japanese-religions.jp/publications/assets/JR35%201%262_Rambelli.pdf Home Buddhas: Historical Processes and Modes of Representation of the Sacred in the Japanese Buddhist Family Altar], Japanese Religions 35 (1–2), 63-86
[[en:Honzon]]
